item.title: mono-alphabetic substitution cipher
item.description: a cipher in which an alphabet is mapped onto another, the key space is any bijection. assume English-language text is being encrypted (i.e., the text is grammatically correct English writing, not just text written using characters of the English alphabet). the mono-alphabetic substitution cipher can then be attacked by utilizing statistical patterns of the English language. (of course, the same attack works for any language.) the attack relies on the facts that:

item.title: shift cipher
item.description: is it possible to recover the message without knowing k? actually, it is trivial! the reason is that there are only 26 possible keys. so one can try to decrypt the ciphertext using every possible key and thereby obtain a list of 26 candidate plaintexts. the correct plaintext will certainly be on this list; moreover, if the ciphertext is “long enough” then the correct plaintext will likely be the only candidate on the list that "

item.title: caesar cipher
item.description: one of the oldest recorded ciphers, known as Caesar’s cipher, is described in De Vita Caesarum, Divus Iulius ("The Lives of the Caesars, the Deified Julius"), written in approximately 110 CE: there are also letters of his to Cicero, as well as to his intimates on private affairs, and in the letter, if he had anything confidential to say, he wrote it in cipher, that is, by so changing the order of the letters of the alphabet, that not a word could be made out…

item.title: encryption scheme
item.description: an encryption scheme is defined by three algorithms , , and , as well as a specification of a (finite) message space with . the key-generation algorithm is a probabilistic algorithm that outputs a key chosen according to some distribution. we denote by the (finite) key space, i.e., the set of all possible keys that can be output by . the encryption algorithm takes as input a key and a message , and outputs a ciphertext .

item.title: private-key encryption scheme
item.description: a private-key encryption scheme is a tuple of probabilistic polynomial-time algorithms such that: the key-generation algorithm takes as input (i.e., the security parameter written in unary) and outputs a key ; we write (emphasizing that is a randomized algorithm). we assume without loss of generality that any key output by satisfies . the encryption algorithm takes as input a key and a plaintext message , and outputs a ciphertext .

item.title: Shannon's law
item.description: for every switching function we get: this law helps us move between DNF and CNF forms without looking at the truth table of a function

item.title: functional completeness
item.description: a set of operations forms a functionally complete system if every switching function can be described as a switching expression that only contains operations from that set for example is functionally complete because and in every switching expression where we have we can replace it with is also functionally complete a functionally complete system consisting of only 1 function returns the complement of a variable if its passed to all its inputs, and the opposite is true, as in a system that doesnt abide by this rule isnt functionally complete

item.title: switching expression
item.description: a switching expression is a finite combination of switching variables and constants by means of switching oeprations . (Kohavi and jha 2010, sec. 3 switching algebra and its applications) a combination of switching expressions results in a switching expression, so if and are both switching expressions then is also a switching expression an example of simplifying a switching expression

item.title: switching algebra
item.description: the basic postulate of switching algebra is the existence of a two-valued switching variable that can take either of two distinct values, 0 and 1. precisely stated, if is a switching variable then these values are often referred to as the truth values of . a switching algebra is an algebraic system consisting of the set , two binary operation called OR and AND, denoted by the symbols + and · respectively, and one unary operation called NOT, denoted by a prime.

item.title: subset
set
item.description: we define a set to be any unordered collection of objects, e.g., is a set. if is an object, we say that is an element of or if lies in the collection; otherwise we say that . for instance, but . (tao 2022, chap. 3 set theory)

item.title: regular language
item.description: a language is called a regular language if some finite automaton recognizes it. (Sipser 2012 definition 1.16) the class of regular languages is closed under the concatenation operation. (Sipser 2012 theorem 1.26) (Sipser 2012 theorem 1.47) the class of regular languages is closed under the star operation. (Sipser 2012 theorem 1.49) the class of regular languages is closed under union. (Sipser 2012 theorem 1.25) (Sipser 2012 theorem 1.45) if is a DFA that recognizes language , swapping the accept and nonaccept states in yields a new DFA recognizing the complement of .

item.title: automata theory course homework 1
item.description: let be languages. prove that one of the following languages is a subset of the other and that they arent equal (give an example in which they differ). intuitively, is the kleene closure of a language with a wider dictionary that contains all of the words of each of the languages or , so it would contain all the strings that those languages contain and strings of words combined from both languages that those languages cannot construct by themselves, this is an informal argument, we write a proof using element chasing.

item.title: independent random variable
item.description: let be an arbitrary index set. for each , let be a measurable space and let be a random variable with generated -algebra . the family of random variables is called independent if the family of -algebras is independent. as a shorthand, we say that a family is "i.i.d." (for "independent and identically distributed") if is independent and if for all . (Klenke 2020 definition 2.14)

item.title: logarithm
item.description: the logarithmic function is the inverse of the exponential function , meaning , defined for log formulas table a collection of logarithm formulas: natural log the natural log is denoted usually by or .

item.title: likelihood
item.description: let be sample observations taken on corresponding random variables whose distribution depends on a parameter . then, if are discrete random variables, the likelihood of the sample, , is defined to be the joint probability of . if are continuous random variables, the likelihood is defined to be the joint density evaluated at . (Dennis Wackerly 2008 definition 9.4)

item.title: probability mass function
item.description: the probability distribution of a discrete random variable.

item.title: bayesian parameter estimation
item.description: we treat both observations (data) and parameters as random variables. we define a model for the joint distribution over parameters and data , to do so we need two things: a distribution , known as the prior distribution. its called the prior because it's supposed to encode your "prioer beliefs", i.e. everything you believe about the parameters before looking at the data. in practice, we normally choose priors to be computationally convenient, rather than based on any sort of statistical principle.

item.title: class

item.title: sigma-algebra
item.description: a class of sets is called a -algebra if it fulfills the following three conditions: . is closed under complements. is closed under countable unions. (Klenke 2020 definition 1.2)

item.title: markov chain
item.description: in the following, is always a Polish space with Borel -algebra and is an -valued stochastic process. we assume that is a filtration generated by . (Klenke 2020, sec. 17 markov chains) we say that has the markov property if, for every and all with , (Klenke 2020 definition 17.1)

item.title: diffusion model
item.description: generative models. diffusion models are latent variable models of the form , where are latents of the same dimensionality as the data . the joint distribution is called the reverse process, and it is defined as a markov chain with learned gaussian transitions starting at : what distinguishes diffusion models from other types of latent variable models is that the approximate posterior , called the forward process or diffusion process, is fixed to a markov chain that gradually adds gaussian noise to the data according to a variance schedule :

item.title: normal distribution
item.description: the normal distribution is a continuous real-valued probability distribution. the density function for a normally distributed random variable that could take the value is we write , the parameter is the expectation (or mean) of the distribution (and is also its median and mode), while the parameter is its standard deviation. the variance of the distribution is . https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Normal_distribution for any and , the function is a probability density on .

item.title: maximum likelihood estimation
item.description: suppose that the likelihood function depends on parameters . choose as estimates those values of the parameters that maximize the likelihood . to emphasize the fact that the likelihood function is a function of the parameters , we sometimes write the likelihood function as . it is common to refer to maximum-likelihood estimators as MLEs. (Dennis Wackerly 2008, sec. 9.7 the method of maximum likelihood) a binomial experiment consisting of trials resulted in observations , where if the th trial was a success and otherwise.

item.title: minkowski loss
item.description: the expectation of the minkowski loss function is given by which reduces to the expected squared loss for . (Bishop 2006, sec. 1.5.5 loss functions for regression)

item.title: estimator
item.description: an estimator is a rule for calculating an estimate of a given quantity based on observed data. for a given sample , the error of the estimator defined as , where is the parameter being estimated. the error, , depends not only on the estimator (the estimation formula or procedure), but also on the sample. the mean squared error of is defined as the expected value (probability-weighted average, over all samples) of the squared errors; that is, .

item.description: let be an interval. a polynomial on is a function of the form , where is an integer and are real numbers. if , then is called the degree of .

item.description: a nondeterministic finite automaton is a 5-tuple , where is a finite set of states, is a finite alphabet, is the transition function, is the start state, is the set of accept states. usually such an automaton is abbreviated NFA. we say an NFA accepts the string if we can write as , where each is a member of and a sequene of states exists in with three conditions:

item.title: deterministic finite automaton
item.description: a finite automaton is a 5-tuple , where is a finite set called the states, is a finite set called the alphabet, is the transition function, is the start state, and is the set of accept states. (Sipser 2012 definition 1.5) a configuration of a finite automaton is an element of . the current configuration of an automaton is a pair (Goldberg, n.d.) given a DFA , for configurations such that , we write iff .

item.description: the closure of a language , denoted by is where denotes the concatenation of the strings . (Sipser 2012 definition 1.23) the closure of a language is denoted and represents the set of those strings that can be formed by taking any number of strings from , possibly with repetitions, and concatenating all of them. (John E. Hopcroft 2006, sec. 3.1.1)

item.title: union of languages
item.description: languages are closed under the union operations. given two languages and , their union is defined as which is the typical definition for the union operation over sets. the class of regular languages is closed under the union operation. in other words, if and are regular languages, so is . (Sipser 2012 theorem 1.25)

item.title: regular expression
item.description: regular expressions differ in syntax from one context to another, and the theory behind them is vaster than i had thought, they are usually studied in automata theory. the operators of regular expressions make use of the following 3 operations on languages: union of languages concatenation of languages closure of a language we call them the regular operations (John E. Hopcroft 2006 definition 1.23) regular expressions are defined inductively:

item.title: automata theory
item.description: automata theory deals with the definitions and properties of mathematical models of computation. these models play a role in several applied areas of computer science. one model, called the finite automaton, is used in text processing, compilers, and hardware design. another model, called the context-free grammar, is used in programming languages and artificial intelligence. automata theory is an excellent place to begin the study of the theory of computation. the theories of computability and complexity require a precise definition of a computer.

item.title: power set
item.description: the power set of a set is the set of all of its subsets.

item.title: language
item.description: a set of strings all of which are chosen from some , where is a particular alphabet, is called a language. if Σ is an alphabet, and , then is a language over . notice that a language over need not include strings with all the symbols of , so once we have established that is a language over Σ, we also know it is a language over any alphabet that is a superset of Σ.

item.title: string
item.description: a string over an alphabet is a finite sequence of symbols from that alphabet. (Sipser 2012) it is often useful to classify strings by their length, that is, the number of positions for symbols in the string. for instance, 01101 has length 5. it is common to say that the length of a string is "the number of symbols" in the string; this statement is colloquially accepted but not strictly correct.

item.title: alphabet
item.description: an alphabet is a finite, non-empty set of symbols. conventionally, we use the symbol Σ for an alphabet. common alphabets include: , the binary alphabet. , the set of all lower-case letters. the set of all ASCII characters, or the set of all printable ASCII characters. (John E. Hopcroft 2006) set operations apply to alphabets. we define to be the set of strings of length , each of whose symbols is in Σ.

item.title: natural
item.description: a natural number is any element of the set which is the set of all the numbers created by starting with 0 and then counting forward indefinitely. we call the set of natural numbers. (tao 2022 definition 2.1.1) in some texts the natural numbers start at 1 instead of 0, but this is a matter of notational convention more than anything else. in (tao 2022) we refer to the set as the positive integers rather than the natural numbers.

item.title: integer
item.description: the set of /integer/s, denoted by , represents whole numbers (numbers without fractions), .

item.title: rational
item.description: denoted by , represents all numbers that can be written as a fraction of 2 integers.

item.title: peano axioms
item.description: 0 is a natural number. if is a natural number, then (its increment) is also a natural number. 0 is not the successor of any natural number; i.e., we have for every natural number . different natural numbers must have different successors: i.e., if are natural numbers and , then . equivalently, if then we must have . let be any property pertaining to a natural number . suppose that is true, and suppose that whenever is true, is also true.

item.title: artificial intelligence homework 5
item.description: (20 pts) use the alpha-beta pruning algorithm to: compute and show the optimal path (explain). show the places you have pruned (explain) explain your process as we did in class! on the right are the values of the pair for a specific state that is associated with the node we're at, which can be deduced by the min/max expressions on the left the step/state number is the rightmost one (in parentheses)

item.title: generative adversarial network
item.description: a generative adversarial network is a pair of networks that combine to form a generate system. one of the networks, the generator, maps values from to in order to produce samples from the distribution . a typical scheme samples from a unit Gaussian of moderate dimension and then passes it through a deep network to obtain . GANs are a kind of implicit model in the sense that samples can be generated but their probabilities are not readily available; the challenge in implicit modeling is to design a loss function that makes it possible to train the model using samples from the distribution, rather than maximizing the likelihood assigned to training examples from the data set.

item.title: manifold parameterization
item.description: parametrization of a manifold a parametrization of a -dimensional manifold is a mapping satisfying the following conditions: is open. is , injective, and onto . is injective for every (JOHN H. HUBBARD 2015) # definition 3.1.18

item.title: monotone function
item.description: a function is strictly monotone if its graph always goes up or always goes down. if always implies , the function is monotone increasing; if always implies , the function is monotone decreasing. (JOHN H. HUBBARD 2015 definition 2.10.1)

item.title: continuously differentiable
item.description: a function is continuously differentiable on if all its partial derivatives exist and are continuous on . such a function is known as function. (JOHN H. HUBBARD 2015 definition 1.9.6)

item.title: ck function
item.description: function a function on is a function that is times continuously differentiable: all of its partial derivatives up to order exist and are continuous on .

item.title: smooth manifold
item.description: smooth manifold in a subset is a smooth -dimensional manifold if locally it is the graph of a mapping expressing variables as functions of the other variables. "locally" means that every point has a neighborhood such that (the part of in ) is the graph of a mapping expressing of the coordinates of each point in in terms of the other . this definition says that if a function is , then its graph is a smooth -dimensional manifold in .

item.title: differentiable
item.description: if is an open subset of , and is a mapping, then is differentiable on , and its derivative is given by its jacobian matrix. (JOHN H. HUBBARD 2015 theorem 1.9.8)

item.title: newton's method
item.description: Newton's method let be a differentiable map from to , where is an open subset of . newton's method consists of starting with some guess ao for a solution of . then linearize the equation at : replace the increment to the function, , by a linear function of the increment, . now solve the corresponding linear equation: this is a system of linear equations in unknowns.

HUBBARD 2015 definition 4.2.3) if the distribution function is of the form for some integrable function , then is called the density of the distribution. (Klenke 2020 definition 1.106)

item.title: jacobian matrix
item.description: let be an open subset of . the jacobian matrix of a function is the matrix composed of the partial derivatives of evaluated at : (JOHN H. HUBBARD 2015 theorem 1.7.10)

item.description: if is a subset of , the closure of , denoted , is the set of such that for all , (JOHN H. HUBBARD 2015 Definition 1.5.8)

item.description: a neighborhood of a point is a subset such that there exists with . (JOHN H. HUBBARD 2015 definition 1.5.7)

item.description: a subset is closed if its complement is open. (JOHN H. HUBBARD 2015 Definition 1.5.4)

HUBBARD 2015) # Definition 1.5.2 the rectangle is open. a set that is not open is not necessarily closed. an open set owns none of its fence. a closed set owns all of its fence.

item.title: substitution
item.description: we denote a substitution by where is a value to be replaced by the value in a given formula

item.title: definite clause
item.description: definite clauses are disjunctions of literals of which exactly one is positive. existential quantifiers are not allowed, and universal quantifiers are left implicit: if you see an in a definite clause, that means there is an implicit quantifier. A typical first-order definite clause looks like this: [page 286 section 9.3.1] in propositional logic, the clause is a definite clause, whereas is not, because it has two positive clauses.

item.title: universal instantiation
item.description: the rule of universal instantiation says that we can infer any sentence obtained by substituting a ground term (a term without variables) for a universally quantified variable. to write out the inference rule formally, we use the notion of substitutions, let denote the result of applying the substitution to the sentence , then the rule is written for any variable and ground term . for example, given the sentence that all greedy kings are evil: we can infer any of the following sentences:

item.title: bear
item.description: from https://emacs-lsp.github.io/lsp-mode/tutorials/CPP-guide/ to understand your source code, clangd needs to know the compiler flags that are used to build the project. (this is just a fact of life in c++, source files are not self-contained). by default, clangd will assume your code is built as clang some_file.cc, and you’ll probably get spurious errors about missing #include files, etc. there are a couple of ways to fix this. you may read about them in clangd installation or in https://sarcasm.

item.title: shellshock
item.description: a vulnerability (CVE-2014-6271) in bash versions 1.14 through 4.3 of the parent process can pass a function definition to a child shell via an environment variable the bash in the child process converts the value of the environment variable to a function so, it is supposed to parse the commands in the variable and not to execute them due to a bug in bash parsing logic, it executes some of the commands in the variable passing a bash function to a child process can be done with

item.title: latent space
item.description: a latent space, is an embedding of a set of items within a manifold in which items resembling each other are positioned closer to one another. position within the latent space can be viewed as being defined by a set of latent variables. it encodes a meaningful internal representation of externally observed events. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Latent_space it is an abstract, lower-dimensional representation of high-dimensional data, often used in machine learning and data science to simplify complex data structures and reveal hidden patterns.

item.title: secure programming homework 3
item.description: first question on env vars step 1, examining the env of the parent process #include <unistd.h> #include <stdio.h> #include <stdlib.h> extern char **environ; void printenv() { int i = 0; while (environ[i] != NULL) { printf("%s\n", environ[i]); i++; } } int main() { pid_t child_pid; switch (child_pid = fork()) { case 0: // child process /* printenv(); */ exit(0); default: // parent process printenv(); exit(0); } } PWD=/home/mahmooz/brain/notes DISPLAY=:0 TEXINPUTS=.:/home/mahmooz/.emacs.d/elpa/auctex-13.2.1/latex: _=/tmp/babel-4G1N4I/C-bin-AKOZ9r OLDPWD=/home/mahmooz/brain/notes QT_SCALE_FACTOR=2 MAIL=/var/spool/mail/mahmooz DBUS_SESSION_BUS_ADDRESS=unix:path=/run/user/1000/bus PATH=/usr/local/sbin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/bin:/usr/bin/site_perl:/usr/bin/vendor_perl:/usr/bin/core_perl BROWSER=firefox DEBUGINFOD_URLS=https://debuginfod.

item.title: constraint satisfaction problem
item.description: refer to [chapter 6] a constraint satisfaction problem consists of three components, , , and : is a set of variables, is a set of domains, , one for each variable is a set of constraints that specify allowable combinations of values a domain, , consists of a set of allowable values, , for variable . for example, a boolean variable would have the domain . different variables can have different domains of different sizes.

item.title: binary search
item.description: a search algorithm goal: given a sorted array and a key, find index of the key in the array method: compare key against middle entry too small, go left too big, go right equal, found time complexity is public static void main(String[] args) { int[] a = {1,10,11,21,30}; System.out.println(binarySearch(a, 10)); } static int binarySearch(int[] a, int key) { int lo = 0, hi = a.length - 1; while (lo <= hi) { int mid = lo + (hi - lo) / 2; if (key < a[mid]) hi = mid - 1; else if (key > a[mid]) lo = mid + 1; else return mid; } return -1; } 1

item.title: zero-sum game
item.description: in a zero-sum game, what is good for one player is just as bad for the other; there is no "win-win" outcome.

item.title: two player zero-sum game
item.description: a deterministic, two-player, turn-taking, perfect information, zero-sum game. "perfect information" is a synonym for "fully observable".

item.title: game tree
item.description: in a game, a game tree is a search tree superimposed over the state space graph, that follows every sequence of moves all the way to a terminal state. the game tree may be infinite if the state space itself is unbounded or if the rules of the game allow for infinitely repeating positions. for tic-tac-toe the game tree is relatively small–fewer than 9! = 362,880 terminal nodes (with only 5,478 distinct states).

item.title: game
item.description: in game theory, a game can be formally defined with the following elements: , the initial state, which specifies how the game is set up at the start. : the player whose turn it is to move in state . : the set of legal movies in state . : the transition model, which defines the state resulting from taking action in state . : a terminal test, which is true when the game is over and false otherwise.

item.title: state space graph
item.description: in a game, the initial state, function, and function, define the state space graph–a graph where the vertices are states, the edges are moves and a state might be reached by multiple paths.

item.title: minimax
item.description: in a two player zero-sum game, we call the two players and . moves first, and then the players take turns moving until the game is over. at the end of the game, points are awarded to the winning player and penalties are given to the loser. given a game tree, the optimal strategy can be determined by working out the minimax value of each state in the tree, which we write as and is defined as: where To-Move returns the player whose turn it is in a given state of the game and Is-Terminal is a predicate that returns whether a state is a terminal state.

item.title: alpha-beta pruning
item.description: alpha-beta pruning is an extension of minimax that uses pruning to reduce the size of the game tree. the general principle of alpha-beta pruning is this: consider a node somewhere in the tree, such that Player has a choice of moving to . if Player has a better choice either at the same level or at any point higher up in the tree, then Player will never move to . so once we have found out enough about (by examining some of its descendants) to reach this conclusion, we can prune it.

item.title: greedy best-firch search
item.description: greedy best-first search is an algorithm that attempts to find the most promising path from a given starting point to a goal by using the evaluation function . the algorithm works by evaluating the cost of each possible path and then expanding the path with the lowest cost. this process is repeated until the goal is reached.

item.title: best-first search
item.description: a general approach to heuristic search is called best-firch search, in which we choose a node in the frontier to expand on, , with the minimum value of some evaluation function, . in that sense, best-first search is a greedy algorithm.

item.title: A* algorithm
item.description: A* (pronounced like "a-star") is a best-first search algorithm that uses the evaluation function where is the path cost from the initial state to node (referring to the path we took to get to which would be the estimated shortest path from the initial state to ), and (heuristic function) is the estimated cost of the shortest path from to a goal state, so we have represents the past knowledge acquired while searching.

item.title: common lisp lparallel
item.description: lparallel is a multithreading library for common lisp, it is useful because the ANSI CL standard doesnt have multithreading it is available via quicklisp: (ql:quickload :lparallel) to get the number of cores we have to use the serapeum package: (ql:quickload :serapeum) usage: (serapeum:count-cpus) 16 in the context of lparallel, a kernel is an abstract entity that schedules and executes tasks. the lparallel kernel API is meant to describe parallelism in a generic manner.

item.title: edge-induced subgraph
item.description: let be an undirected graph, and let . an edge-induced subgraph is a graph that contains the edges and all the vertices at the endpoints of these edges. let be a connected undirected graph, assume that bfs has been applied to starting at the vertex . let be the edge-induced subgraph with the edges the resulting tree is a distance tree relative to in . with the help of this bfs lemma: each vertex in the graph is found once, so has edges and vertices, for each vertex , in there would be a path from to , so is a tree by definition.

item.title: complete graph

item.title: multivariate linear regression
item.description: (Peter Norvig 2020) we can easily extend from univariate linear regression to multivariate linear regression, such that each example is an n-element vector. our hypothesis space is the set of functions of the form the term, the intercept, stands out as different from the others. we can fix that by inventing a dummy input attribute, , which is defined as always equal to 1. then is simply the dot product of the weights and the input vector, or equivalently the matrix product of the transpose of the weights and hte input vector: the best vector of weights, minimizes squared-error loss over the examples: here we have a continuous weight space and we cannot find a closed-form solution.

item.title: univariate linear regression
item.description: (Peter Norvig 2020) a univariate linear function (a straight line) with input and output has the form , where and are real-valued coefficients to be learned. we use the letter because we think of the coefficients as weights; the value of is changed by changing the relative weight of one term or another. we’ll define to be the vector , and define the task of finding the that best fits these data is called linear regression.

item.title: entropy
item.description: cite: (Peter Norvig 2020) entropy is a measure of uncertainty of a random variable a random variable with only one value–a coin that always comes up heads–has no uncertainty and thus its entropy is defined as zero; thus, we gain no information by observing its value. a flip of a fair coin is equally likely to come up heads or tails, 0 or 1, this counts as “1 bit” of entropy.

item.title: supervised learning
item.description: cite:&aima2010 the task of supervised learning is this: given a training set of example input-output pairs , where each was generated by an unknown function , discover a function that approximates the true function . here and can be any value; they need not be numbers. the function is a hypothesis from a hypothesis space, denoted by , learning is a search through the space of possible hypotheses for one that will perform well, even on new examples beyond the training set.

item.title: heap sort
item.description: heap sort consists of transforming the given array into a heap array A, initiating heap-size to size(A) then applying the following steps recursively: replace the root of the heap A[0] with A[heap-size - 1] bubble down the newly replaced root (A[0]) decrement heap-size, return to step 1 if heap-size > 1 the time complexity of this algorithm is on the third step when decrementing heap-size we arent deleting the last element, we are simply decreasing the size of the part of the list that we're operating on because the part at the end contains elements that are sorted

item.title: merge sort
item.description: yet another divide-and-conquer algorithm given an array, this algorithm keeps splitting it until it reaches a subarray that cant be divided which happens when a subarray contains only 1 or 0 elements, each of these subarrays are sorted individually and then combined, recursively, to eventually make a larger sorted array refer to merge algorithm this algorithm runs in time image taken from https://www.programiz.com/dsa/merge-sort, will keep this here until i make my own

item.title: bubble sort
item.description: this algorithm repeatedly steps through the input list element by element, comparing the current element with the one after it, swapping their values if needed. these passes through the list are repeated until no swaps had to be performed during a pass, meaning that the list has become fully sorted. the algorithm, which is a comparison sort, is named for the way the larger elements "bubble" up to the top of the list.

item.title: merge algorithm

item.title: insertion sort
item.description: iterate from arr[1] to arr[n] compare the current element to its predecessor if the current element is smaller than its predecessor, drag it backwards until its bigger than its predecessor time complexity is template <typename T> void insertion_sort(T arr[], int len) { for (int i = 1; i < len; ++i) { int idx = i; while (idx > 0 && arr[idx] < arr[idx-1]) { T tmp = arr[idx]; arr[idx] = arr[idx-1]; arr[idx-1] = tmp; idx--; } } }

item.title: selection sort
item.description: selection sort maintains two subarrays in a given array, one subarray that is sorted and the other unsorted, it sorts a given array by repeatedly finding the minimum element (considering ascending order) from the unsorted part and putting it at the beginning in every iteration of selection sort, the minimum element (considering ascending order) from the unsorted subarray is picked and moved to the sorted subarray. time complexity is template <typename T> void selection_sort(T arr[], int len) { for (int i = 0; i < len; ++i) { // find index of minimum in unsorted subarray int min_idx = i; for (int j = i+1; j < len; ++j) { if (arr[j] < arr[min_idx]) { min_idx = j; } } // add minimum to the sorted subarray T tmp = arr[i]; arr[i] = arr[min_idx]; arr[min_idx] = tmp; } } example usage:

item.title: comparison sort

item.title: partition algorithm
item.description: an algorithm that given an array, puts a given element, which we call a pivot, in its correct position in the array by placing all the elements that are bigger than it after it, and all the elements that are smaller than it before it

item.title: common lisp math utils
item.description: essential functions (defun sigmoid (x) (cond ((< x -70) 0) ;; to avoid float overflow (t (/ 1 (1+ (exp (- x))))))) (defun sigmoid-derivative (x) (* (sigmoid x) (- 1 (sigmoid x)))) (defun relu (x) (max x 0)) (defun relu-derivative (x) "derivative of relu is undefined at x=0, but here we return 0 for x=0 too" (if (> x 0) 1 0)) vectors these functions work, but some use lists and some do redundant operations, they need efficiency improvement

item.title: common lisp utils
item.description: arrays ;; (defun array-width (2d-array) ;; (car (cdr (array-dimensions 2d-array)))) ;; (defun array-height (2d-array) ;; (car (array-dimensions 2d-array))) (defun array-rows (arr) "number of rows in array" (elt (array-dimensions arr) (- (length (array-dimensions arr)) 2))) (defun array-cols (arr) "number of columns in array" (elt (array-dimensions arr) (- (length (array-dimensions arr)) 1))) (defun array-depth (arr) "depth of a tensor of the third order" (elt (array-dimensions arr) (- (length (array-dimensions arr)) 3))) (defun array-size (arr) (array-total-size arr)) turn multidimensional (nested) list into array:

item.title: decision tree
item.description: (Peter Norvig 2020) a decision tree represents a function that takes as input a vector of attribute values and returns a "decision"–a single output value. the input and output values can be discrete or continuous for a wide variety of problems, the decision tree yields a nice, concise result. but some functions cannot be represented concisely, for example, the majority function, which returns true if and only if more than half of the inputs are true, requires an exponentially large decision tree.

item.title: discrete math
feedforward neural network
item.description: feedforward neural networks is a wider term than discussed here, whats discussed here really is a specific type we call a simple multilayer perceptron, but this serves as an introductory to other types of more complex networks a feedforward network has connections only in one direction. each node computes a function of its inputs and passes the result to its successors in the network. information flows through the network from the input nodes to the output nodes, and there are no loops.

item.title: forward chaining
item.description: (Peter Norvig 2020, sec. 7.5.4) a family of inference algorithms propositional logic chaining the forward-chaining algorithm (which stands for "propositional logic forward chaining entails" and is shown in fig:fig2) determines if a single proposition symbol –the query–is entailed by a knowledge base of definite clauses. it begins from known facts (positive literals) in the knowledge base. if all the premises of an implication are known, then its conclusion is added to the set of known facts.

item.title: trees in common lisp
item.description: (see trees in lisp) (defun tree-to-latex-forest (tree) "turn a tree into a runnable tikz block" (format t "\\begin{forest}") (tree-to-latex-forest-recurse tree) (format t "\\end{forest}")) (defun tree-to-latex-forest-recurse (tree) "helper function for tree-to-latex-forest" (if (not (eq tree nil)) (progn (format t "[~a,circle,draw" (car tree)) (loop for child in (cdr tree) do (tree-to-latex-forest-recurse child)) (format t "]")) (format t "[,phantom]")) tree) (let ((my-tree '(24 (12) (12 (1 nil (4 nil (5 nil (9 nil (10 nil nil))))) nil) (28 nil (45 nil (89 nil nil)))))) (tree-to-latex-forest my-tree))

item.title: unification
item.description: the Unify algorithm (shown in fig:fig1) takes two sentences and returns a unifier for them (a substitution) if one exists: let us look at some examples of how Unify should behave. suppose we have a query : whom does John know? answers to this query can be found by finding all sentences in the knowledge base that unify with . here are the results of unification with four different sentences that might be in the knowledge base:
